name: Permutations and Combinations — Counting Cleverly
whatIsIt: This is the art of counting arrangements and selections without listing them all. A FACTORIAL n! = n × (n−1) × … × 1 counts full arrangements. A PERMUTATION nPr = n!/(n−r)! counts ordered arrangements (order matters). A COMBINATION nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!) counts selections (order does NOT matter). By definition 0! = 1.
whyNeeded: From seating people, forming teams, setting passwords, to lottery odds — you need to count possibilities fast. Permutations handle 'in how many orders', combinations handle 'in how many ways to choose', the backbone of probability and computer science.
realLifeUses: How many ways to arrange books on a shelf (permutations)
How many teams of 2 from a group (combinations)
The number of possible PINs or passwords

vocabulary.meaning: Arrangements of r items from n where ORDER matters: n!/(n−r)!.
Selections of r items from n where order does NOT matter: n!/(r!(n−r)!).
The key question: is ABC different from CBA? If yes → permutation; if no → combination.

story.title: The Ice-Cream Choice
story.text: The stall has 5 flavours. 'How many ways can I pick 2 different scoops in a cup, where order doesn't matter?' asks Sana. Bhaiya says that's a combination: 5C2 = 5!/(2! × 3!) = (5 × 4)/(2 × 1) = 10 ways. 'But if I stacked them in a cone where the bottom scoop matters, it becomes 5P2 = 20 ways,' Sana realises. Order changes everything!
story.extractedProblem: In how many ways can 2 scoops be chosen from 5 flavours if order does not matter?

visual.caption: 5 choices for the first place × 4 for the second = 20 ordered arrangements (5P2). If order doesn't matter, halve to get 5C2 = 10.

teachingGallery.title: Order matters? Then permutation
teachingGallery.note: Same items, different order = different permutation but the SAME combination.

examples.caption: 3 items arrange in 3! = 6 orders: ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA.
Each combination of 2 has 2! = 2 orders, so permutations are twice the combinations here.

standardMethod.summary: Decide if order matters. Order matters → nPr = n!/(n−r)!. Order doesn't → nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!). Use n! = n×(n−1)×…×1, and 0! = 1.
steps: Ask: does the order of the chosen items matter?
Compute factorials, cancelling common parts to keep numbers small.
Check: nCr should be smaller than nPr for the same n and r (order ignored means fewer).

commonMistakes.mistake: Using a permutation when order does not matter (over-counting).
commonMistakes.fix: If order does NOT matter, use nCr — divide by r! to remove the repeated orders. Teams and groups are combinations.
By definition 0! = 1 (there is exactly one way to arrange nothing).

challenge.q: Choosing a team of 3 from 10 people uses…
hintLadder: A team is a group, not a line.

teachBackPrompt: Show a grown-up how choosing 2 ice-cream scoops from 5 is 5C2 = 10 when order doesn't matter, but 5P2 = 20 when it does. Explain n! and that 0! = 1.

revisionCard.summary: n! = n×(n−1)×…×1, and 0! = 1. Order matters → permutation nPr = n!/(n−r)!. Order doesn't matter → combination nCr = n!/(r!(n−r)!). Teams/groups are combinations; arrangements/PINs are permutations.

rememberIt.hook: Order matters -> Permutation (P). Order doesn't -> Combination (C)
rememberIt.unpack: nPr = n!/(n-r)!. nCr = n!/(r!(n-r)!). C is P divided by r!, because we do not count the r! re-orderings.

studentQuestions.q: How do I know P or C?
studentQuestions.a: Ask: does the ORDER matter? A race podium (1st, 2nd, 3rd) -> permutation. Picking a team of 3 -> combination (order does not matter).

studentQuestions.q: Why divide by r! for combinations?
studentQuestions.a: Every group of r can be arranged in r! orders. Permutations count all of them; combinations count the group once - so divide by r!.

formulas.name: Combinations
formulas.remember: Order does not matter = nPr / r!
